reference: GATE CS — Compiler Design / Frontend Phases

map.condition: Need FIRST set of a string or production body
Need FOLLOW set of a non-terminal

map.use: \text{LL(1): no ambiguity, no left recursion, no common prefix; } |\text{SELECT}(A\to\alpha) \cap \text{SELECT}(A\to\beta)| = 0

map.condition: Resolving operator precedence / associativity in shift-reduce conflict

formulas.name: FIRST Set Definition
formulas.latex: \text{FIRST}(\alpha) = \{ a \in T \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^* a\beta \} \cup \begin{cases} \{\varepsilon\} & \text{if } \alpha \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon \\ \emptyset & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: FIRST(alpha) = set of terminals that can begin a string derived from alpha; include epsilon if alpha can derive epsilon
formulas.whenToUse: Building LL(1) parse table entries and SELECT sets
formulas.trap: FIRST of a non-terminal A is NOT the same as FIRST of every production of A — you must union FIRST of all RHS bodies

formulas.name: FIRST Computation Rules
formulas.latex: \begin{aligned} &\text{1. } a \in T \Rightarrow \text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\} \\ &\text{2. } A \to \varepsilon \Rightarrow \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(A) \\ &\text{3. } A \to X_1 X_2 \ldots X_k:\; \text{add FIRST}(X_i)\setminus\{\varepsilon\} \text{ while } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(X_1)\ldots\text{FIRST}(X_{i-1}) \end{aligned}
formulas.plain: Rule 1: FIRST(terminal) = {terminal}. Rule 2: epsilon-production adds epsilon. Rule 3: for A -> X1 X2 ... Xk, add FIRST(Xi) - {epsilon} as long as all previous Xi can derive epsilon
formulas.whenToUse: Computing FIRST sets step-by-step for any grammar
formulas.trap: Stop propagating when you hit a symbol that cannot derive epsilon; beginners forget to remove epsilon from intermediate FIRST sets

formulas.name: FOLLOW Set Definition & Rules
formulas.latex: \begin{aligned} &\text{1. } \$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S) \quad (S = \text{start symbol}) \\ &\text{2. } B \to \alpha A \beta :\; \text{FIRST}(\beta)\setminus\{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(A) \\ &\text{3. } B \to \alpha A \text{ or } (B \to \alpha A \beta,\, \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)):\; \text{FOLLOW}(B) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(A) \end{aligned}
formulas.plain: FOLLOW(A): 1) $ in FOLLOW(start). 2) If B -> alpha A beta, add FIRST(beta)-{epsilon} to FOLLOW(A). 3) If beta can derive epsilon (or absent), add FOLLOW(B) to FOLLOW(A)
formulas.whenToUse: Computing FOLLOW sets; deciding where to put epsilon-production rows in LL(1) table

terms.means: string following A in production body

formulas.trap: Never include epsilon in a FOLLOW set; FOLLOW sets contain only terminals and $

formulas.name: SELECT (Predict) Set
formulas.latex: \text{SELECT}(A \to \alpha) = \begin{cases} (\text{FIRST}(\alpha)\setminus\{\varepsilon\}) \cup \text{FOLLOW}(A) & \text{if } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha) \\ \text{FIRST}(\alpha) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: SELECT(A -> alpha) = FIRST(alpha) - {epsilon} union FOLLOW(A) when alpha can derive epsilon; else just FIRST(alpha)
formulas.whenToUse: Building LL(1) parse table: entry M[A, a] gets production A->alpha whenever a in SELECT(A->alpha)
formulas.trap: Confusing SELECT with FIRST; they differ exactly when alpha can derive epsilon

formulas.name: LL(1) Grammar Condition
formulas.latex: \text{Grammar is LL(1) iff for every two distinct productions } A \to \alpha \mid \beta:\\[4pt] \text{SELECT}(A\to\alpha) \cap \text{SELECT}(A\to\beta) = \emptyset
formulas.plain: Grammar is LL(1) iff for any two alternatives A -> alpha | beta, their SELECT sets are disjoint
formulas.whenToUse: Deciding if a grammar admits a deterministic top-down parser without backtracking

terms.means: two distinct alternative bodies for non-terminal A

formulas.trap: An unambiguous grammar without left recursion or common prefix is NECESSARY but not SUFFICIENT — you must still verify SELECT sets

formulas.name: Left Recursion Elimination
formulas.latex: A \to A\alpha \mid \beta \;\Longrightarrow\; A \to \beta A',\quad A' \to \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon
formulas.plain: A -> A alpha | beta  becomes  A -> beta A'  and  A' -> alpha A' | epsilon
formulas.whenToUse: Making a grammar suitable for top-down (LL) parsing by removing immediate left recursion

terms.means: rest of recursive production after A

formulas.trap: Indirect left recursion (A -> B alpha, B -> A beta) must be handled by ordering and substitution first

formulas.name: Ambiguous Grammar Definition
formulas.latex: \text{A grammar } G \text{ is ambiguous if } \exists\, w \in L(G) \text{ with two distinct leftmost derivations (or parse trees)}
formulas.plain: Grammar G is ambiguous if some string has more than one leftmost derivation (equivalently, more than one parse tree)
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying ambiguity — construct two different parse trees for the same sentence

terms.means: witness string that has two parse trees

formulas.trap: Ambiguity is a property of the grammar, NOT the language; the same CFL may have an unambiguous grammar

formulas.name: Dangling Else — Classic Ambiguity
formulas.latex: S \to \textbf{if}\; e\; \textbf{then}\; S \mid \textbf{if}\; e\; \textbf{then}\; S\; \textbf{else}\; S \mid \text{other}
formulas.plain: S -> if e then S | if e then S else S | other  -- the 'else' can match the nearest or farthest 'if', making the grammar ambiguous
formulas.whenToUse: Recognizing the dangling-else grammar as a canonical ambiguous grammar example
formulas.trap: Resolution rule 'else matches nearest unmatched if' is a semantic convention — the grammar itself remains ambiguous

formulas.name: LR Parser Power Hierarchy
formulas.latex: \text{LR(0)} \subsetneq \text{SLR(1)} \subsetneq \text{LALR(1)} \subsetneq \text{CLR(1) (Canonical LR)} \subsetneq \text{CFG}
formulas.plain: LR(0) < SLR(1) < LALR(1) < CLR(1)  (strictly increasing parsing power; all are subsets of all CFLs)
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing parser strengths; a grammar rejected by SLR may still be LALR(1) or CLR(1)

terms.means: strictly subset — more grammars accepted as we go right

formulas.trap: SLR, LALR and CLR(1) all have the SAME NUMBER of states for the same grammar — only the lookahead sets differ

formulas.name: LR(0) Item
formulas.latex: [A \to \alpha \bullet \beta] \quad (\text{dot marks how much of production has been recognized})
formulas.plain: [A -> alpha . beta]  where dot shows the current parse position; alpha on stack, beta still to read
formulas.whenToUse: Building the LR(0) automaton (canonical collection of sets of LR(0) items)
formulas.trap: [A -> alpha .] is a REDUCE item; any non-dot position is a SHIFT item — do not confuse the two

formulas.name: SLR(1) Reduce Rule
formulas.latex: \text{SLR(1) reduce } [A \to \alpha \bullet] \text{ on lookahead } a \iff a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)
formulas.plain: In SLR(1): reduce by A -> alpha when the next input token 'a' is in FOLLOW(A)
formulas.whenToUse: Filling the action table for an SLR(1) parser; resolving shift-reduce conflicts

terms.means: set of terminals that can follow A in any sentential form

formulas.trap: SLR(1) uses global FOLLOW sets; conflicts arise when FOLLOW(A) overlaps FIRST(beta) for another item in the same state

formulas.name: LALR(1) vs CLR(1) Items
formulas.latex: \begin{aligned} &\text{CLR(1) item: } [A \to \alpha \bullet \beta,\; a] \quad (a = \text{lookahead terminal}) \\ &\text{LALR(1): merge CLR(1) states with same core}\; (\text{same LR(0) items, different lookaheads}) \end{aligned}
formulas.plain: CLR(1) keeps full lookahead per item. LALR(1) merges states that share the same LR(0) core, unioning their lookaheads. Same number of states as LR(0)/SLR(1).
formulas.whenToUse: Understanding why LALR(1) can have reduce-reduce conflicts not present in CLR(1) after merging
formulas.trap: Merging LALR(1) states can introduce reduce-reduce conflicts; it NEVER introduces shift-reduce conflicts that were not already in CLR(1)

formulas.name: Shift-Reduce & Reduce-Reduce Conflicts
formulas.latex: \begin{aligned} &\text{Shift-Reduce (S/R): state has both } [A \to \alpha \bullet a \beta] \text{ and } [B \to \gamma \bullet] \text{ for same lookahead } a \\ &\text{Reduce-Reduce (R/R): state has two complete items } [A \to \alpha \bullet] \text{ and } [B \to \beta \bullet] \text{ with overlapping lookaheads} \end{aligned}
formulas.plain: Shift-Reduce: can either shift 'a' or reduce B->gamma. Reduce-Reduce: two different reductions applicable on same lookahead — always a grammar defect
formulas.whenToUse: Diagnosing parse table conflicts; R/R conflicts are more serious than S/R
formulas.trap: LR(0) grammar has NO conflicts at all; SLR(1) resolves some but not all S/R conflicts that LR(0) cannot

formulas.name: ACTION and GOTO Table Structure
formulas.latex: \begin{aligned} &\text{ACTION}[s, a] \in \{\text{shift}\; s',\; \text{reduce}\; A\to\alpha,\; \text{accept},\; \text{error}\} \\ &\text{GOTO}[s, A] = s'' \quad (A \in N,\; s'' = \text{state after reducing to } A) \end{aligned}
formulas.plain: ACTION table: indexed by (state, terminal). GOTO table: indexed by (state, non-terminal). GOTO is identical across SLR(1), LALR(1), and CLR(1) for the same grammar.
formulas.whenToUse: Constructing or reading LR parse tables; GOTO part is never source of ambiguity
formulas.trap: The GOTO tables of SLR(1) and LALR(1) are identical; only ACTION entries may differ

formulas.name: Operator Precedence Relations
formulas.latex: \begin{aligned} &a \lessdot b :\; a \text{ yields precedence to } b \;(\text{shift } b) \\ &a \doteq b :\; a \text{ and } b \text{ have equal precedence} \\ &a \gtrdot b :\; a \text{ takes precedence over } b \;(\text{reduce before shifting } b) \end{aligned}
formulas.plain: a <. b : shift b (b has higher precedence). a =. b : same precedence. a >. b : reduce a before shifting b (a has higher precedence or left-associative at same level)
formulas.whenToUse: Building an operator-precedence parsing table for expression grammars

terms.means: yields precedence (left side has lower precedence)
takes precedence (left side has higher or equal with left-assoc)

formulas.trap: Operator-precedence parsing does NOT build a standard parse tree; it handles only operator grammars (no two adjacent non-terminals)

formulas.name: Precedence from Grammar Structure
formulas.latex: E \to E * F \mid F + E \mid F \implies * \text{ has higher precedence than } +
formulas.plain: In expression grammars, an operator appearing deeper in the grammar (i.e., closer to the leaves/atoms) has higher precedence. * is deeper than + so * binds tighter.
formulas.whenToUse: Reading precedence and associativity directly from a given expression grammar without a precedence table

terms.means: non-terminals at different levels of the grammar hierarchy

formulas.trap: Associativity is encoded by recursion direction: E -> E op F is left-associative, E -> F op E is right-associative

formulas.name: Top-Down vs Bottom-Up Parsing
formulas.latex: \begin{aligned} &\text{Top-Down (LL): leftmost derivation, expands non-terminals} \\ &\text{Bottom-Up (LR): rightmost derivation in reverse, reduces terminals to non-terminals} \end{aligned}
formulas.plain: LL parsers build parse tree from root to leaves (top-down, leftmost). LR parsers build from leaves to root (bottom-up, reverse rightmost derivation).
formulas.whenToUse: Classifying parsing strategies; LL(k) uses k tokens of lookahead top-down, LR(k) uses k tokens bottom-up
formulas.trap: LL parsers cannot handle left recursion; LR parsers can handle more grammars but are harder to build by hand

formulas.name: Handle in Bottom-Up Parsing
formulas.latex: \text{A handle of a right-sentential form } \gamma \text{ is a production } A \to \beta \text{ and position in } \gamma \text{ such that } \gamma = \alpha \beta w \text{ and } S \Rightarrow^*_{rm} \alpha A w
formulas.plain: Handle = the substring beta to be reduced next (matching some production A -> beta) such that reducing it continues a valid rightmost derivation in reverse
formulas.whenToUse: Formalizing what a shift-reduce parser reduces at each step

terms.means: current right-sentential form on stack + remaining input

formulas.trap: Handles are always found at the TOP of the stack; never in the remaining input portion

formulas.name: LL(1) Parse Table Construction
formulas.latex: M[A, a] \gets A \to \alpha \quad \text{for each } a \in \text{SELECT}(A \to \alpha)
formulas.plain: Add production A -> alpha to table cell M[A, a] for every terminal a in SELECT(A -> alpha). If any cell gets two productions, grammar is not LL(1).
formulas.whenToUse: Constructing the LL(1) parsing table from SELECT sets

terms.means: parse table cell for non-terminal A and lookahead terminal a

formulas.trap: An epsilon-production A -> epsilon is added in row A for all a in FOLLOW(A), not FIRST(epsilon)

formulas.name: Viable Prefix
formulas.latex: \text{A viable prefix is a prefix of a right-sentential form that does not extend past the rightmost handle}
formulas.plain: Viable prefix = any stack content that can appear during a valid LR parse; the LR automaton recognizes exactly the set of viable prefixes
formulas.whenToUse: Understanding what LR(0) items/automaton recognizes; every state in the LR automaton is a set of viable-prefix items

terms.means: rightmost reducible substring in current sentential form

formulas.trap: Not every prefix of a right-sentential form is a viable prefix — it must not go past the handle
